Name: Declaring April 28 as Willie Colón Day.
Title: Resolution declaring April 28 as Willie Colón Day in the City of New York to honor the multi-talented trombone player, bandleader, composer, and producer, who was a New York City pioneer of Latin music and a beloved salsa legend
BodyName: Committee on Cultural Affairs, Libraries and International Relations

History.Description: This Resolution was Introduced by Council
This Resolution was Referred to Comm by Council to the Committee on Cultural Affairs, Libraries and International Relations
History.AgendaNote: Cultural Affairs, Libraries and International Relations

Text:
Whereas, William Anthony Col�n Rom�n was born in the South Bronx on April 28, 1950, and raised by his Puerto Rican grandmother, who encouraged his interest in music; and
      Whereas, Known professionally as Willie Col�n, he started playing trumpet at the age of 12, but switched to the trombone two years later and began working professionally while still a teenager; and
      Whereas, At 16, he began following his mentor and collaborator bandleader-composer-trombonist Mon Rivera to nightclubs, where Col�n was often invited up to play with the band; and
      Whereas, Col�n described the new salsa sound he helped create, which was influenced by R&B, jazz, and Caribbean dance music, as "rebellious music" forged in the days of civil rights unrest and as music that "wasn't explicitly political...but was a magnet that would bring people together," according to an interview in The Miami Herald; and
      Whereas, Col�n's first album, El Malo, recorded for Johnny Pacheco's Fania label in 1967 at the age of 17, featured Puerto Rican vocalist H�ctor Lavoe and began their prolific collaboration of two decades; and
      Whereas, For decades that followed the album, Col�n enjoyed performing in the "bad boy" persona that emerged from El Malo; and
      Whereas, Col�n's 1978 album Siembra, part of a long and productive partnership with Panamanian singer and songwriter Rub�n Blades, became one of the best-selling salsa albums of all time and produced the masterpiece seven-minute single "Pedro Navaja," described by Rolling Stone magazine as "an existential tale of Latino gangsters, out of luck prostitutes, and carefree drunkards-with references to Kafka and Kurt Weill, and a sardonic chorus: la vida tiene sorpresas (life is full of surprises)"; and
      Whereas, Col�n went on to record more albums with Blades, including Canciones del Solar de los Aburridos (1981), which produced hit singles like "Tiburon," "Ligia Elena," and "Te Estan Buscando"; and
      Whereas, Col�n also played with Latin music superstars Celia Cruz, the Cuban-born queen of salsa, and musician-bandleader-songwriter-Tito Puente, among others; and
      Whereas, Col�n was nominated for 10 GRAMMY Awards across two decades for best Latin recording and best tropical Latin performance for 10 different albums, including Canciones del Solar de los Aburridos, Corazon Guerrero, Criollo, Especial No. 5, Altos Secretos, Color Americano, Hecho en Puerto Rico, and Tras la Tormenta; and
      Whereas, Col�n received The Latin Recording Academy's lifetime achievement award in 2004 and was inducted into the Latin Songwriters Hall of Fame in 2019; and 
      Whereas, In 2015, Billboard magazine named Col�n one of the 30 most influential Latin artists of all time, noting that he was "instrumental in creating the sound that became known as salsa," that he "did much to cultivate the streetwise urban Latino image adopted by artists today," and that "his album El Malo is a classic testimony to the Nuyorican sound and style of the '70s"; and
      Whereas, When Rolling Stone magazine named the 50 greatest salsa albums of all time, Col�n had produced or performed on six of them, including the number one selection, Siembra, and Asalto Navide�o, the best-ever Christmas Latin music record, which produced the hit single "La Murga"; and
      Whereas, During his long career, Col�n made more than 40 albums, including nine that went gold and five that went platinum; and
      Whereas, According to Rolling Stone magazine, singles that "serve as an introduction to [Col�n's] genius," stretch from 1969's "Che Che Col�," an adaptation of a children's song from Ghana; to 1981's "Oh Qu� Ser�," the beginning of the salsa rom�ntica sound; to 1993's "Idilio," a "late-career smash," which "boasts fiery trombone mo�as and Willie's vocals in rare form"; and
      Whereas, Long interested in politics, Col�n ran unsuccessfully in a 1994 Democratic primary for a Bronx-Westchester County seat in the United States House of Representatives and later worked for New York City Mayor Michael Bloomberg as a liaison to the Latin Media and Entertainment Commission; and
      Whereas, Col�n died on February 21, 2026, at the age of 75 and was remembered by his family, friends, and fans at a public funeral mass at St. Patrick's Cathedral on March 9, 2026; and
      Whereas, In a Facebook post announcing Col�n's death, his manager Pietro Carlos wrote that Col�n's "trombone was the voice of the people"; and
      Whereas, Col�n took his ever-evolving brand of salsa from the South Bronx to worldwide acclaim; now, therefore, be it
	Resolved, That the Council of the City of New York declares April 28 as Willie Col�n Day in the City of New York to honor the multi-talented trombone player, bandleader, composer, and producer, who was a New York City pioneer of Latin music and a beloved salsa legend.

\pard\ltrpar\sl480\slmult1\qj By Council Members Far\'edas, Aldebol, Cab\'e1n, J. Sanchez, Avil\'e9s, Encarnaci\'f3n and Hudson\par
\pard\ltrpar\fi720\sl480\slmult1\qj Whereas, William Anthony Col\'f3n Rom\'e1n was born in the South Bronx on April 28, 1950, and raised by his Puerto Rican grandmother, who encouraged his interest in music; and\par
Whereas, Known professionally as Willie Col\'f3n, he started playing trumpet at the age of 12, but switched to the trombone two years later and began working professionally while still a teenager; and\par
Whereas, At 16, he began following his mentor and collaborator bandleader-composer-trombonist Mon Rivera to nightclubs, where Col\'f3n was often invited up to play with the band; and\par
Whereas, Col\'f3n described the new salsa sound he helped create, which was influenced by R&B, jazz, and Caribbean dance music, as \ldblquote rebellious music\rdblquote  forged in the days of civil rights unrest and as music that \ldblquote wasn\rquote t explicitly political\'85but was a magnet that would bring people together,\rdblquote  according to an interview in \i The Miami Herald\i0 ; and\par
Whereas, Col\'f3n\rquote s first album, \i El Malo\i0 , recorded for Johnny Pacheco\rquote s Fania label in 1967 at the age of 17, featured Puerto Rican vocalist H\'e9ctor Lavoe and began their prolific collaboration of two decades; and\par
Whereas, For decades that followed the album, Col\'f3n enjoyed performing in the \ldblquote bad boy\rdblquote  persona that emerged from \i El Malo\i0 ; and\par
Whereas, Col\'f3n\rquote s 1978 album \i Siembra\i0 , part of a long and productive partnership with Panamanian singer and songwriter Rub\'e9n Blades, became one of the best-selling salsa albums of all time and produced the masterpiece seven-minute single \ldblquote Pedro Navaja,\rdblquote  described by \i Rolling Stone\i0  magazine as \ldblquote an existential tale of Latino gangsters, out of luck prostitutes, and carefree drunkards-with references to Kafka and Kurt Weill, and a sardonic chorus: \i la vida tiene sorpresas \i0 (life is full of surprises)\rdblquote ; and\par
Whereas, Col\'f3n went on to record more albums with Blades, including \i Canciones del Solar de los Aburridos\i0  (1981), which produced hit singles like \ldblquote Tiburon,\rdblquote  \ldblquote Ligia Elena,\rdblquote  and \ldblquote Te Estan Buscando\rdblquote ; and\par
Whereas, Col\'f3n also played with Latin music superstars Celia Cruz, the Cuban-born queen of salsa, and musician-bandleader-songwriter-Tito Puente, among others; and\par
Whereas, Col\'f3n was nominated for 10 GRAMMY Awards across two decades for best Latin recording and best tropical Latin performance for 10 different albums, including \i Canciones del Solar de los Aburridos\i0 ,\i  Corazon Guerrero\i0 ,\i  Criollo\i0 ,\i  Especial No. 5\i0 ,\i  Altos Secretos\i0 ,\i  Color Americano\i0 , \i Hecho en Puerto Rico\i0 , and\i  Tras la Tormenta\i0 ; and\par
Whereas, Col\'f3n received The Latin Recording Academy\rquote s lifetime achievement award in 2004 and was inducted into the Latin Songwriters Hall of Fame in 2019; and \par
Whereas, In 2015, \i Billboard\i0  magazine named Col\'f3n one of the 30 most influential Latin artists of all time, noting that he was \ldblquote instrumental in creating the sound that became known as salsa,\rdblquote  that he \ldblquote did much to cultivate the streetwise urban Latino image adopted by artists today,\rdblquote  and that \ldblquote his album \i El Malo\i0  is a classic testimony to the Nuyorican sound and style of the \rquote 70s\rdblquote ; and\par
Whereas, When \i Rolling Stone\i0  magazine named the 50 greatest salsa albums of all time, Col\'f3n had produced or performed on six of them, including the number one selection, \i Siembra\i0 , and \i Asalto Navide\'f1o\i0 , the best-ever Christmas Latin music record, which produced the hit single \ldblquote La Murga\rdblquote ; and\par
Whereas, During his long career, Col\'f3n made more than 40 albums, including nine that went gold and five that went platinum; and\par
Whereas, According to \i Rolling Stone\i0  magazine, singles that \ldblquote serve as an introduction to [Col\'f3n\rquote s] genius,\rdblquote  stretch from 1969\rquote s \ldblquote Che Che Col\'e9,\rdblquote  an adaptation of a children\rquote s song from Ghana; to 1981\rquote s \ldblquote Oh Qu\'e9 Ser\'e1,\rdblquote  the beginning of the \i salsa rom\'e1ntica\i0  sound; to 1993\rquote s \ldblquote Idilio,\rdblquote  a \ldblquote late-career smash,\rdblquote  which \ldblquote boasts fiery trombone \i mo\'f1as\i0  and Willie\rquote s vocals in rare form\rdblquote ; and\par
Whereas, Long interested in politics, Col\'f3n ran unsuccessfully in a 1994 Democratic primary for a Bronx-Westchester County seat in the United States House of Representatives and later worked for New York City Mayor Michael Bloomberg as a liaison to the Latin Media and Entertainment Commission; and\par
Whereas, Col\'f3n died on February 21, 2026, at the age of 75 and was remembered by his family, friends, and fans at a public funeral mass at St. Patrick\rquote s Cathedral on March 9, 2026; and\par
Whereas, In a Facebook post announcing Col\'f3n\rquote s death, his manager Pietro Carlos wrote that Col\'f3n\rquote s \ldblquote trombone was the voice of the people\rdblquote ; and\par
Whereas, Col\'f3n took his ever-evolving brand of salsa from the South Bronx to worldwide acclaim; now, therefore, be it\par
\pard\ltrpar\sl480\slmult1\qj\tab Resolved, That the Council of the City of New York declares April 28 as Willie Col\'f3n Day in the City of New York to honor the multi-talented trombone player, bandleader, composer, and producer, who was a New York City pioneer of Latin music and a beloved salsa legend.\par
